html:
[402]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 愿父母徐以待之 - Polite request for parents to be patient and wait for the child to find their own path.
  • 俟儿自觅缘 - Wait for the child to find their own fate or destiny.
  • 或者得偿夙愿 - Perhaps they can fulfill their long-held wishes.
  • 容未可知也 - It remains to be seen.
  • 止此弱息 - Only this frail offspring, referring to the couple's only child.
  • 不忍拂其意 - Unable to go against their wishes.
  • 见字俯从 - Agreed to the letter's request.
  • 遂罢执柯之使 - Thus ended the matchmaker's efforts.
  • 明年，一鸣已十七 - The following year, Yi Ming was seventeen.
  • 父又将商于他省 - The father was about to go into business in another province.
  • 盍从父出游 - Why not go with the father on a trip?
  • 少识关山之路 - To gain some knowledge of the roads through mountains and passes.
  • 纵以哑废 - Even if he becomes mute and disabled.
  • 犹可以商世其家 - He can still carry on the family business.
  • 不愈于默坐井中 - It is not worse than sitting silently in a well.
  • 箕裘以堕哉 - The family legacy will not be lost.
  • 一鸣欣然就道 - Yi Ming happily set out on the journey.
  • 所过名山川，辄有题咏 - Whenever passing through famous mountains and rivers, he would write poems.
  • 其尤脍炙人口者 - Those especially well-liked and widely praised.
  • 莫如函谷关一律 - None more so than the poem about the Hangu Pass.
  • 雄镇固金汤 - A strong fortress, as solid as a golden cauldron and a moat of molten iron.
  • 耽耽视六王 - Intimidatingly watching over the six kings.
  • 地吞百越尽 - Its territory swallowing up all of the Baiyue.
  • 祚翦二周长 - Its mandate cutting short the two Zhou dynasties.
  • 雉眫存余烈 - The remains of the former glory still linger.
  • 丸泥少异方 - The ball of mud slightly differs from other places.
  • 青牛背上客 - Refers to Laozi, the legendary founder of Taoism, who is said to have left the capital riding on an ox.
  • 长笑过咸阳 - Laughing loudly as he passed through Xianyang.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 函谷关 - Refers to the Hangu Pass, a strategic pass in ancient China.
  • 六王 - Refers to the six states that were conquered by the Qin dynasty.
  • 百越 - Refers to the various tribes and states in southern China.
  • 二周 - Refers to the Western Zhou and Eastern Zhou periods.
  • 青牛背上客 - Refers to Laozi, the founder of Taoism, who is said to have left the capital riding on an ox.
  • 咸阳 - Refers to Xianyang, the capital of the Qin dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 一鸣 - The name of the child, Yi Ming.
  • 函谷关 - The Hangu Pass.
  • 六王 - The six states conquered by the Qin.
  • 百越 - The various tribes and states in southern China.
  • 二周 - The Western Zhou and Eastern Zhou periods.
  • 青牛背上客 - Laozi, the founder of Taoism.
  • 咸阳 - Xianyang, the capital of the Qin dynasty.

[403]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以筆代舌 - Using a pen instead of a tongue, meaning to write rather than speak.
  • 人罔知一鳴不鳴也 - No one knows whether One Cry is speaking or not, a play on the name 'One Cry' and the ambiguity of silence or speech.
  • 未諳趨避 - Not yet adept at evading or avoiding, referring to One Cry's lack of experience in navigating the river.
  • 波浪兼天 - Waves reaching the sky, a hyperbolic description of the height of the waves.
  • 帆樯上下 - Sails and masts moving up and down, describing the chaotic motion of the ship in the storm.
  • 立身不住 - Unable to stand firm, indicating One Cry's inability to maintain balance.
  • 順流而下 - Going downstream, describing the direction of the ship's movement.
  • 風威稍霁 - The wind's power slightly abates, indicating the storm is weakening.
  • 冥搜 - Searching in the dark or searching blindly, referring to the difficulty of finding One Cry in the vast river.
  • 招魂設祭 - Summoning the soul and setting up a ritual, a traditional practice to mourn the dead.
  • 痛哭而返 - Crying bitterly and returning, describing the father's emotional reaction.
  • 南游之志 - The intention to travel south, referring to the father's original plan to go to Hankou.
  • 心膽皆落 - Heart and courage both fall, a metaphor for extreme fear.
  • 不作再生想 - Not thinking of being reborn, indicating despair or resignation.
  • 少飲西江 - Drinking a little from the West River, a metaphor for the vastness of the river.
  • 遽沉水底 - Suddenly sinking to the bottom, describing One Cry's death.
  • 捉生替死者 - Those who catch the living to replace the dead, a mysterious and possibly supernatural element.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 一鳴 - The name 'One Cry' is likely a play on the idiom '一鳴驚人' (to make a startling debut), but here it is used ironically to highlight the character's silence.
  • 淮上 - Refers to the area around the Huai River, a significant geographical feature in Chinese history.
  • 漢口 - Refers to Hankou, one of the three towns that make up modern-day Wuhan, an important city in China.
  • 瓜步 - Refers to the area around the Guazhou Ferry, a famous crossing point on the Yangtze River.
  • 十八姨 - A legendary figure in Chinese folklore, often associated with wind and storms. In this context, it refers to the storm that causes the shipwreck.
  • 烏有 - A place name meaning 'nowhere,' signifying the absence of One Cry's body.
  • 招魂設祭 - A traditional Chinese ritual to summon the soul of the deceased and perform a memorial service.
  • 痛哭而返 - Reflects the deep emotional impact of losing a child, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 一鳴 - The name of the character, likely a play on the idiom '一鳴驚人.'
  • 淮上 - The area around the Huai River.
  • 漢口 - The city of Hankou.
  • 瓜步 - The area around the Guazhou Ferry.
  • 十八姨 - A legendary figure associated with wind and storms.
  • 烏有 - A place name meaning 'nowhere.'

[404]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 哗曰 - Colloquial expression meaning 'the crowd shouted' or 'people exclaimed,' indicating a collective reaction.
  • 代予者至矣 - Means 'the one to replace me has arrived,' suggesting a transition or substitution.
  • 竹冠布衣 - Literal description of a bamboo hat and cloth robe, commonly worn by Taoist priests or recluses.
  • 携杖疾趋 - Means 'carrying a staff and walking quickly,' indicating the道士's swift and purposeful movement.
  • 熟视一鸣而骇曰 - Means 'looked at closely and was startled, saying,' describing the道士's reaction.
  • 此哑进士也 - Refers to 'this mute scholar,' a term that may carry specific connotations or references in the context.
  • 汝曹意将奚为 - Rhetorical question meaning 'What do you intend to do?' directed at a group.
  • 乃叱散群鬼 - Means 'then he shouted and dispersed the ghosts,' indicating a supernatural or symbolic action.
  • 握一鸣之腕 - Means 'grasped the wrist of one who was mute,' suggesting a physical interaction.
  • 分水徐行 - Means 'divided the water and walked slowly,' describing a magical or controlled movement.
  • 江皆壁立 - Means 'the river stood like a wall,' indicating a dramatic or supernatural transformation.
  • 由此而西 - Means 'from here to the west,' indicating a direction.
  • 自有佳境 - Means 'there will be a fine place,' suggesting a positive outcome.
  • 探囊中 - Means 'searched his bag,' indicating a physical action.
  • 以一卷相授 - Means 'gave a scroll to him,' suggesting a transfer of knowledge or power.
  • 素女之术 - Refers to 'the techniques of the Lady Su,' a legendary figure associated with Taoist alchemy or sexual practices.
  • 与汝卫身 - Means 'to protect you,' indicating a purpose.
  • 非诲淫也 - Means 'not to teach lust,' clarifying the intent.
  • 须慎用之 - Means 'must be used with caution,' indicating a warning.
  • 言已不见 - Means 'after speaking, he disappeared,' indicating a supernatural departure.
  • 甫经奇险 - Means 'just experienced strange dangers,' indicating a recent event.
  • 神魂瞀乱 - Means 'mind and spirit were confused,' indicating a state of disorientation.
  • 不能问 - Means 'could not ask,' indicating a physical or mental limitation.
  • 即欲问亦不能言 - Means 'even if he wanted to ask, he could not speak,' indicating a further limitation.
  • 有心志之而已 - Means 'only had the intention,' indicating a mental state.
  • 手不敢释 - Means 'could not let go,' indicating a strong attachment or reverence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest or hermit, often associated with supernatural abilities.
  • 竹冠布衣 - A common attire for Taoist priests, symbolizing simplicity and asceticism.
  • 素女 - A legendary figure in Taoist texts, often associated with sexual techniques or alchemy.
  • 哑进士 - A term that may refer to a scholar who is mute, possibly a metaphor for someone who is misunderstood or marginalized.
  • 群鬼 - Refers to a group of ghosts, often associated with supernatural or symbolic threats.
  • 江皆壁立 - A dramatic description of a river standing like a wall, possibly a metaphor for a powerful or insurmountable obstacle.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces a collective reaction and a statement about a replacement, which must be read together to understand the context.
  • This long sequence describes the道士's appearance and actions, which must be read as a single, continuous description.
  • This sequence describes a series of actions and their effects, which must be read as a single, dramatic event.
  • This sequence describes the道士's guidance and the promise of a positive outcome, which must be read as a single, coherent instruction.
  • This sequence describes the道士's gift and instructions, which must be read as a single, coherent exchange.
  • This sentence indicates the道士's departure, which must be read as a single, dramatic event.
  • This sequence describes the主人公's state after the event, which must be read as a single, coherent description.
  • This sentence describes the主人公's reaction to the gift, which must be read as a single, coherent statement.

Proper Nouns

  • 道士 - A Taoist priest or hermit.
  • 哑进士 - A term that may refer to a scholar who is mute.
  • 素女 - A legendary figure in Taoist texts.
  • 群鬼 - A group of ghosts.

[405]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 幸天时盛暑 - Phrase meaning 'fortunately, the weather was hot,' indicating a favorable condition despite the heat.
  • 衣履淋漓尚无所苦 - Literal meaning 'clothes and shoes soaked but no discomfort,' suggesting that despite being wet, the person was not bothered.
  • 一鸣衣水而前 - Describes the action of 'one person wearing wet clothes and moving forward.'
  • 犹忆道士之教 - Means 'still remembering the teachings of the Taoist,' referring to a memory of advice or instruction.
  • 不复向东 - Means 'no longer heading east,' indicating a change in direction.
  • 行未里许 - Means 'after walking about a li (a unit of distance),' describing the distance covered.
  • 见有巨宅一区 - Means 'saw a large estate,' describing the sight of a grand building.
  • 势甚壮丽 - Means 'very magnificent,' describing the appearance of the estate.
  • 一鸣素不习步 - Means 'one person who was not accustomed to walking,' indicating a lack of experience in walking.
  • 甫及墙阴 - Means 'just reached the shadow of the wall,' describing the position relative to the wall.
  • 吻张足软 - Means 'mouth open, legs weak,' describing a state of exhaustion or fatigue.
  • 因小憩于树下 - Means 'rested briefly under a tree,' describing a short rest.
  • 翘首四望 - Means 'looked around curiously,' describing the act of looking in all directions.
  • 正当坐处有垝垣 - Means 'there was a dilapidated wall where one was sitting,' describing the location of a damaged wall.
  • 因雨未及修整者 - Means 'because of the rain, it had not been repaired,' explaining why the wall was in a poor state.
  • 窥之 - Means 'peered at it,' describing the act of looking closely.
  • 绿莎茸茸 - Means 'green grass lush and soft,' describing the appearance of the grass.
  • 别无花木 - Means 'no other flowers or trees,' indicating the absence of additional vegetation.
  • 恍似人家废圃 - Means 'seemed like an abandoned garden,' describing the overall impression.
  • 一鸣少年鲁莽 - Means 'one person, a young and impulsive individual,' describing the character's age and personality.
  • 顿思藉此为晾衣地 - Means 'immediately thought of using it as a place to dry clothes,' describing a sudden idea.
  • 竟踉跄而起 - Means 'finally stumbled to his feet,' describing the act of getting up despite weakness.
  • 乘缺以入内 - Means 'took advantage of the gap to enter inside,' describing the method of entering.
  • 果阒其无人 - Means 'indeed, no one was there,' confirming the absence of people.
  • 有一亭 - Means 'there was a pavilion,' describing the structure.
  • 茅覆其顶 - Means 'covered with thatch on top,' describing the roof material.
  • 四围皆植瓜蔬 - Means 'surrounded by vegetables,' describing the surroundings.
  • 其前又别有崇墉 - Means 'in front, there was another high wall,' describing the additional wall.
  • 高竹半出 - Means 'tall bamboo partially emerging,' describing the bamboo plants.
  • 茂树拂垣 - Means 'lush trees touching the wall,' describing the trees near the wall.
  • 知为主人游观之所 - Means 'knew it was the place for the owner to enjoy,' describing the purpose of the area.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist practitioner, indicating a spiritual or philosophical influence.
  • 巨宅 - Refers to a large estate or mansion, common in Chinese literature to describe grand residences.
  • 垝垣 - Refers to a dilapidated wall, often used to describe neglected or abandoned structures.
  • 绿莎 - Refers to green grass, often associated with natural beauty and tranquility in Chinese poetry.
  • 废圃 - Refers to an abandoned garden, often used to evoke a sense of neglect or decay.
  • 高竹 - Refers to tall bamboo, often associated with elegance and refinement in Chinese culture.
  • 茂树 - Refers to lush trees, often used to describe a natural and serene environment.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is clear but could be interpreted as emphasizing the favorable weather despite the heat.
  • This sequence describes the character's actions and thoughts, with a change in direction.
  • This describes the character's journey and the sight of a grand estate.
  • This describes the character's physical state and actions.
  • This describes the character's observation and the state of the wall.
  • This describes the character's close inspection and the overall impression.
  • This describes the character's impulsive decision and actions.
  • This describes the detailed features of the area.

[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一鸣审视良久 - Yi Ming examined carefully for a long time.
  • 乃即亭边尽去衣缕 - Then he immediately went to the pavilion side and removed all his clothes.
  • 摊而曝之 - Laid them out to dry.
  • 计俟燥而后行 - Planning to wait until they were dry before proceeding.
  • 惊定神疲 - After the shock wore off, his spirit was exhausted.
  • 不耐危坐 - Unable to sit upright for long.
  • 遂自身卧于檐际 - So he lay down on the eaves.
  • 倦极思眠 - Tired to the point of wanting to sleep.
  • 一时不觉熟寐 - Momentarily unaware that he had fallen into a deep sleep.
  • 半枕初回 - Halfway through his sleep, he woke up.
  • 娇音群噪 - A group of sweet voices began to chirp.
  • 从婢数人 - Several maidservants.
  • 皆怒曰 - All shouted angrily.
  • 何处莽儿郎 - Where did this rude young man come from?
  • 敢裸裎寄人庑下 - Dared to be naked under someone else's roof?
  • 一鸣不能出声 - Yi Ming could not make a sound.
  • 惟以手示之意 - Only showing his intention with his hand.
  • 众乃笑曰 - The crowd then laughed and said.
  • 是儿固生而哑者也 - This child was indeed born mute.
  • 美人微睨其具 - The beautiful lady glanced at his equipment.
  • 意似不胜歆羡 - Seemed to be unable to contain her admiration.
  • 忽与婢耳语数四 - Suddenly whispered to the maidservant several times.
  • 婢皆有笑容 - The maidservants all had smiles on their faces.
  • 美人遽反身曰 - The beautiful lady quickly turned around and said.
  • 羞杀阿侬 - Shameful for me.
  • 目既为人所污矣 - My eyes have already been tainted by someone.
  • 遂冉冉先去 - And slowly left first.
  • 一鸣惧祸 - Yi Ming feared trouble.
  • 将起着衣而奔 - Got up, dressed, and ran away.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 亭边 - Refers to the side of a pavilion, a common feature in classical Chinese literature often associated with scenic or secluded places.
  • 衣缕 - Refers to clothing, specifically the threads or fabric that make up garments.
  • 曝之 - Refers to drying clothes in the sun, a common practice in ancient times.
  • 危坐 - Refers to sitting upright, often used to describe a formal or dignified posture.
  • 檐际 - Refers to the eaves of a building, often used in classical literature to describe a place of rest or shelter.
  • 娇音群噪 - Refers to a group of sweet voices, often used to describe birds or other small creatures.
  • 从婢数人 - Refers to several maidservants, common in classical literature to describe the household staff.
  • 美人 - Refers to a beautiful lady, often used in classical literature to describe a woman of high social status or beauty.
  • 羞杀阿侬 - Refers to a term of endearment used by the beautiful lady, indicating a close relationship or familiarity.
  • 目既为人所污矣 - Refers to the idea of being tainted or defiled, often used in classical literature to describe a loss of purity or innocence.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 直前曳其臂 - Literally means 'to pull her arm straight forward,' describing a sudden and forceful action.
  • 娘子怒汝唐突 - '娘子' is a respectful term for a woman, often used by servants. '唐突' means 'rude' or 'impertinent.' The phrase indicates that the woman is angry because the character has acted rudely.
  • 将白主翁治汝罪 - '白' means 'to report,' and '主翁' means 'master' or 'mistress.' The phrase means 'to report to the master to punish you.'
  • 汝乃思遁耶 - '思遁' means 'to think of escaping.' The phrase is a rhetorical question, implying that the character is trying to escape.
  • 又一婢坌息而来曰 - '坌息' means 'panting' or 'out of breath,' indicating that the second servant is rushing in.
  • 娘子命将贼去，施敲扑矣 - '贼' means 'thief' or 'criminal,' and '敲扑' means 'beating' or 'punishment.' The phrase means 'the mistress has ordered to take the thief away and punish him.'
  • 众婢皆笑 - '众婢' means 'all the maids,' and '皆笑' means 'all laughed.' The phrase indicates that the maids are laughing at the situation.
  • 推挽以行 - '推挽' means 'pushing and pulling,' describing the maids' actions in guiding the character.
  • 心少安 - '心少安' means 'his heart felt a little more at ease.' The phrase indicates that the character feels less anxious.
  • 反扃其户 - '反扃' means 'locked from the inside,' indicating that the room was locked.
  • 以此为裩，不消着裤裤矣 - '裩' is a type of undergarment, and '不消着裤裤' means 'no need to wear pants.' The phrase is a playful and ironic comment, suggesting that the room is so nice that the character doesn't need to wear anything more.
  • 仙言所谓佳境者 - '仙言' means 'words of the immortals' or 'mythical advice,' and '佳境' means 'beautiful place' or 'ideal setting.' The phrase refers to the character's previous belief that this place is a paradise.
  • 顿悟 - '顿悟' means 'sudden enlightenment' or 'realization,' indicating that the character suddenly understands the true nature of the situation.
  • 屏息俟之 - '屏息' means 'hold one's breath,' and '俟之' means 'wait for it.' The phrase describes the character's state of anticipation.
  • 日即暮，婢以榼至 - '日即暮' means 'the day is almost over,' and '婢以榼至' means 'a maid arrived with a jug.' The phrase sets the scene for the evening.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 娘子 - A respectful term for a woman, often used by servants. It reflects the hierarchical and polite nature of servant-mistress relationships in traditional Chinese society.
  • 主翁 - Refers to the master or mistress of the household, emphasizing the authority and status of the person in charge.
  • 敲扑 - A form of physical punishment, reflecting the strict discipline and control in traditional Chinese households.
  • 仙言 - Refers to the character's previous belief in a mythical or idealized setting, which is now being questioned.
  • 佳境 - A term often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a beautiful or ideal place, often associated with immortals or mythical beings.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 直前曳其臂曰 - This phrase is a bit ambiguous. It could mean 'pulled her arm straight forward and said' or 'pulled her arm and said straight forward.' The context suggests the former.
  • 娘子命将贼去，施敲扑矣 - This phrase can be read as two linked actions: 'the mistress has ordered to take the thief away and punish him.'
  • 以此为裩，不消着裤裤矣 - This phrase is a playful and ironic comment, suggesting that the room is so nice that the character doesn't need to wear anything more. The structure is somewhat elliptical, but the meaning is clear.
  • 仙言所谓佳境者 - This phrase can be read as a reference to the character's previous belief in a mythical or idealized setting, which is now being questioned.

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 推户入 - Literal phrase meaning 'push the door open and enter,' used to describe an action of entering a place.
  • 娘子虑汝饿损 - Madam was worried that you would starve, indicating concern for the well-being of the person being addressed.
  • 不任鞭箠 - Cannot bear punishment or corporal punishment.
  • 以此与汝果腹 - With this to satisfy your hunger, providing food to satisfy hunger.
  • 坦然举箸 - Calmly pick up chopsticks, indicating the act of eating without hesitation.
  • 娘子已不胜酒力 - Madam cannot hold her liquor, indicating that the woman is drunk.
  • 归即思睡 - As soon as she returns, wants to sleep, indicating a desire to sleep.
  • 牵一鸣出暗室 - Pull One-ming out of a dark room, suggesting leading someone out of a dark room.
  • 闺中椒兰纷郁 - In the boudoir, fragrant herbs are abundant, indicating a room filled with the scent of fragrant herbs.
  • 银烛荧煌 - Silver candles shine brightly, describing candles that shine brightly.
  • 绣帷锦衾 - Embroidered curtains and brocade quilts, indicating luxurious and beautiful bedding.
  • 大好福 - Great good fortune, expressing a sense of happiness or luck.
  • 坦腹此中 - Lie flat in here, indicating lying down in a comfortable place.
  • 不愈于荒亭露宿耶 - Not better than sleeping in a desolate pavilion, suggesting that lying in a comfortable place is not better than sleeping in a desolate pavilion.
  • 衾枕香软 - Bedding is fragrant and soft, describing the comfort and pleasantness of the bedding.
  • 纱烛一对 - A pair of gauze candles, referring to a pair of gauze candles.
  • 痴老子不羞 - Foolish old man is not ashamed, suggesting that the old man is not embarrassed.
  • 苦缠阿侬酒 - Persistently纠缠 with alcohol, indicating persistent纠缠 with alcohol.
  • 几误侬好事矣 - Almost ruined a good thing, suggesting that almost ruined a good thing.
  • 裸体儿安在 - Where is the naked child, indicating the location of the naked child.
  • 已在衾中 - Already in the bedding, indicating that the child is already in the bedding.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 娘子 - A term of address for a woman, often used in a respectful or affectionate manner.
  • 鞭箠 - Refers to whips and rods, suggesting punishment or corporal punishment.
  • 银烛荧煌 - Describes candles that shine brightly, indicating a luxurious and elegant setting.
  • 绣帷锦衾 - Indicates luxurious and beautiful bedding, suggesting a high-status living environment.
  • 纱烛一对 - Refers to a pair of gauze candles, indicating a refined and elegant setting.
  • 痴老子 - A colloquial term for an old man, often used in a derogatory or affectionate manner.
  • 阿侬 - A colloquial term for 'I' or 'me,' indicating a personal and intimate context.
  • 裸体儿 - Refers to a naked child, suggesting a vulnerable and unprotected state.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The door was pushed open, and wine and food were given to him. She smiled and said, 'Madam was worried that you would starve, and couldn't bear the punishment, so she gave you this to satisfy your hunger. You can eat.' One-ming knew there was no danger and calmly picked up his chopsticks. The maid laughed and said, 'This place is poisoned, and you still dare to eat?' One-ming ignored her. After eating, the maid took the leftovers away. One-ming still lay naked, thinking of his father, and secretly shed tears. Later, after three drums had struck, he heard the maids talking, 'Madam can't hold her liquor anymore, and she wants to sleep. You can go to bed first.' They then led One-ming out of the dark room. The boudoir was filled with the scent of fragrant herbs, and the silver candles shone brightly. The embroidered curtains and brocade quilts were so beautiful that they couldn't be described. The group led One-ming to the bed and said, 'Great good fortune, lie flat here, is it not better than sleeping in a desolate pavilion?' One-ming lay down and felt the fragrant and soft bedding, which made him feel relaxed. After some time, a pair of gauze candles appeared, guiding a beautiful woman into the room. She said to herself, 'Foolish old man is not ashamed, persistently纠缠 with me and almost ruined my good thing.' She then asked the maid, 'Where is the naked child?' The maid replied, 'He is already in the bedding.'

Proper Nouns

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '美人腼然随去，俄顷归房，眉目似有喜色' describes the beauty leaving and returning with a happy expression.
  • The sequence '挽一鸣就坐，裣皀启曰' describes the beauty leading the young man to sit and speaking politely.
  • The sequence '主翁将有所浼，郎其勿辞' describes the master asking for something and the young man being asked not to refuse.
  • The sequence '以手询之，美人亦以耳语之，两俱欣幸' describes the young man asking with his hand and the beauty whispering back, both being happy.
  • The sequence '欲为杜易女装，免人涉疑，一鸣亦颔之，无难色' describes the beauty wanting to change into female attire to avoid suspicion, and the young man agreeing without hesitation.
  • The sequence '大悦，乃命婢报翁曰' describes the beauty being very happy and sending a maid to report to the master.
  • The sequence '事谐矣，少顷即易弁为钗来见也' describes the matter being settled and the beauty changing into female attire to come and see.
  • The sequence '绾髻，施朱粉，添芗泽' describes the beauty tying up her hair, applying makeup, and adding fragrance.
  • The sequence '妆犹未竟，忽闻帘间笑语' describes the beauty's makeup not being finished when she hears laughter and conversation behind the curtain.
  • The sequence '偷花贼既已败露，羞杀淫婢子矣' describes the lover being exposed and the beauty being shamed.
  • The sequence '分我一杯羹，不愈于独吃此吓耶' describes the idea of sharing benefits and the question of whether it is better to share or keep them all.

  • 有能續成者，吾即事之 - Phrase indicating that anyone who can complete the poem will marry her; '續成' means 'complete,' and '即事之' means 'will marry.'
  • 直指取其詩以示紳士 - Phrase indicating that the official showed the poem to the scholars; '紳士' refers to scholars or gentlemen.
  • 踵之者凡數十人，悉揮去 - Phrase indicating that many people tried to continue the poem but were all dismissed; '踵之者' means 'those who followed,' and '悉揮去' means 'all dismissed.'
  • 後聞一鳴能詩，召使續之 - Phrase indicating that the official later heard that one person could write poetry and summoned them to complete the poem; '一鳴' refers to the person who could write poetry.
  • 意固有在也 - Phrase indicating that the official had a specific intention; '意固有在也' means 'had a specific intention.'
  • 一鳴援筆立成曰 - Phrase indicating that the person took up the brush and completed the poem immediately; '援筆' means 'took up the brush,' and '立成' means 'completed immediately.'
  • 落月屋梁眠自穩，飛花簾幕舞偏高 - Line from the poem indicating that the moon sets on the beam and the person sleeps peacefully, while the flying flowers dance high on the curtain; '落月' means 'setting moon,' and '飛花' means 'flying flowers.'
  • 女覽之喜曰 - Phrase indicating that the daughter was pleased upon reading the poem; '覽之' means 'read it,' and '喜曰' means 'said happily.'
  • 郎在是矣 - Phrase indicating that the man is here; '郎在是矣' means 'the man is here.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - Conventional opening indicating the author or commentator is addressing the reader directly.
  • 人之英华多因言泄 - Literally means 'the brilliance of a person often leaks through words,' referring to the idea that a person's inner qualities can be revealed through speech.
  • 哑则克全其天 - 'If one is mute, one can preserve one's natural state.' This suggests that not speaking can help maintain one's original nature.
  • 声色当前，真元剥蚀 - 'When sounds and colors are present, the true essence is eroded.' This implies that external stimuli can diminish one's inner purity.
  • 一鸣惊人 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to make a great impression with a single action' or 'to achieve sudden fame.'
  • 不若不鸣之为愈也 - 'It is better not to speak at all.' This suggests that silence is preferable to sudden fame.
  • 守口如瓶 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to keep one's mouth closed like a bottle,' emphasizing the importance of silence.
  • 徒贻笑口 - 'To be laughed at in vain.' This implies that one's efforts are futile if they lead to ridicule.
  • 不亦哀哉 - 'Is it not sad?' This rhetorical question expresses sorrow or pity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 外史氏 - A term often used in classical Chinese texts to refer to a historian or commentator, suggesting a reflective or didactic tone.
  • 随园老人 - Reference to Yuan Mei (袁枚), a famous Qing dynasty poet and scholar who used the pen name '随园老人' (Old Man of Sui Garden).
  • 蕊榜 - Refers to the imperial examination results, specifically the list of successful candidates.
  • 云逵 - Metaphorical expression referring to the path to success or high office, often used in the context of the imperial examinations.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence structure is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the polite inquiry.
  • The metaphorical expression needs to be rendered in a way that captures both the literal and figurative meanings.
  • The sequence of actions (turning, wanting to return, falling) must be clearly separated in English.
  • The cause-and-effect relationship between the sweat dissipating and running must be clear.

  • 好物弗坚 - A philosophical or moral expression meaning 'fine things are not enduring' or 'good things do not last.'

  • 怎步金莲 - '金莲' is a term for a woman’s foot, often used in classical literature to describe small, delicate feet. The phrase '怎步' means 'how can one walk,' suggesting the difficulty or impossibility of walking with such feet.
  • 黄土伤心 - '黄土' is 'yellow earth' or 'soil,' symbolizing burial or death. '伤心' is 'heart-wrenching' or 'saddening,' used to express sorrow over death.
  • 银瓶固哀 - '银瓶' is a 'silver bottle' or 'vessel,' possibly a metaphor for a woman or a symbol of beauty. '固哀' is 'surely mournful' or 'certainly sorrowful.'
  • 落井青燐 - '落井' is 'falling into a well,' a metaphor for death. '青燐' is 'blue flame' or 'ghostly light,' referring to the soul or spirit after death.
  • 怵目翠钿 - '怵目' is 'startling to the eye' or 'shocking to the sight.' '翠钿' is 'emerald inlay' or 'green ornament,' possibly referring to jewelry or decorations on a woman’s body.

  • 风霾黑黑 - '风霾' is 'wind and haze,' used metaphorically for darkness or gloom. '黑黑' is 'black-black,' emphasizing the darkness.
  • 夜台无再返之香魂 - '夜台' is 'night platform' or 'underworld,' a metaphor for the afterlife. '香魂' is 'fragrant soul,' referring to the spirit of a beautiful woman.

  • 莲萼值凋零之日 - '莲萼' is 'lotus calyx,' used as a metaphor for the female genitalia or a symbol of beauty. '凋零' is 'withering' or 'fading.'
  • 剥面不仅无皮 - '剥面' is 'peeling the face,' '无皮' is 'no skin,' suggesting the degradation of the body after death.
  • 横波当立涸之时 - '横波' is 'horizontal wave,' used to describe the eyes. '立涸' is 'instantly dry,' referring to the drying up of the eyes after death.
  • 刳目何尝有肉 - '刳目' is 'to carve out the eyes,' '何尝有肉' is 'how could there be flesh,' suggesting that the eyes are empty or hollow after death.
  • 香温玉软 - '香温' is 'fragrant and warm,' '玉软' is 'soft as jade,' describing the softness and fragrance of a woman’s body.
  • 鸡肋根根 - '鸡肋' is a metaphor for something that is not worth much, like a chicken rib. '根根' is 'each one,' suggesting that the body is reduced to such insignificant remains.
  • 脸杏腮桃 - '杏腮' is 'apricot cheeks,' '桃腮' is 'peach cheeks,' both used to describe a woman’s rosy face.
  • 瓠犀落落 - '瓠犀' is 'gourd teeth,' a poetic term for teeth, often used to describe small, white, even teeth. '落落' is 'scattered' or 'falling.'
  • 烧残劫火 - '劫火' is 'conflagration of the world-destroying cycles' (from Buddhist cosmology), '烧残' is 'burning away.'
  • 蝉鬓飞烟 - '蝉鬓' is 'cicada-like hair,' referring to a woman’s hair that is thin or delicate. '飞烟' is 'flying smoke,' suggesting the hair is dissolving or disappearing like smoke.
  • 贴近寒泉 - '寒泉' is 'cold spring,' a metaphor for death or burial.
  • 雪肤化水 - '雪肤' is 'snow-white skin,' '化水' is 'turn to water,' suggesting the body is melting away after death.
  • 玉环飞燕 - '玉环' is 'jade ring,' a reference to *Yang Guifei*, the beloved concubine of Emperor Xuanzong. '飞燕' is 'flying swan,' a reference to *Zhang Huaijin*, the famous dancer and concubine of Emperor Wu of Han. These are allusions to beautiful women of history.
  • 红粉骷髅 - A common classical expression meaning 'a beautiful woman’s skeleton,' a metaphor for the transience of beauty and the inevitability of death.
  • 花蕊梅精 - '花蕊' is 'flower pistil,' '梅精' is 'plum spirit,' both used metaphorically to refer to beautiful women or spirits.
  • 绿窗满目 - '绿窗' is 'green window,' a symbol of a woman’s chamber or a place of beauty. '满目' is 'filling the eyes,' suggesting that the room is full of beauty.
  • 兴言及此，奚为掩卷神驰 - '兴言' is 'to speak of,' '奚为' is 'why,' '掩卷' is 'close the book,' '神驰' is 'mind wanders.' The phrase suggests that upon hearing or reading this, the reader cannot help but be transported in thought.
  • 结想于斯，无事披图意乱 - '结想' is 'to imagine or think deeply,' '披图' is 'to look at a picture,' '意乱' is 'mind confused.' The phrase suggests that thinking about this is enough to cause emotional disturbance, even without seeing an image.
  • 倩女久成兰麝土 - '倩女' is a beautiful woman, '兰麝' is 'fragrant herbs,' '土' is 'earth.' The phrase suggests that the beautiful woman has turned to earth.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 子頓悟 - The son suddenly realized or understood, indicating a sudden epiphany or realization.
  • 未嫁而孕 - Unmarried and pregnant, a phrase that carries both social and moral connotations in classical Chinese society.
  • 父母怒缢之 - Parents, angry, strangled her, a direct but emotionally charged expression of punishment.
  • 草葬 - Buried in the grass or buried in a shallow grave, indicating a hasty or dishonorable burial.
  • 大怖而奔归 - Greatly terrified and ran back home, indicating a strong emotional reaction and physical movement.
  • 犹慴然，股肉战战弗宁 - Still trembling, his leg muscles shaking and not at peace, describing a state of extreme fear or anxiety.
  • 诘辍耕故 - Inquired about the reason for abandoning farming, a direct but formal way of asking about the cause of an action.
  • 以实告 - Told the truth, indicating honesty or candor.
  • 汝惰农自安，以妖异相诳耶 - You are lazy in farming and seek comfort, using strange and supernatural things to deceive me? A rhetorical question expressing disbelief and accusation.
  • 驱之使就庐舍 - Forced him to go to the hut, indicating coercion or assignment.
  • 不予之归 - Did not allow him to return home, indicating refusal or prohibition.
  • 自计 - Calculated to himself or thought to himself, indicating internal reflection.
  • 鬼若来此，诚无地可避 - If the ghost comes here, truly there is no place to hide, expressing a sense of inevitability or helplessness.
  • 盍纳之，少识裙下乐 - Why not accept her, to know a little of the joy beneath the skirt? A metaphor for sexual pleasure or intimacy.
  • 死亦无憾 - Even to die, there would be no regret, indicating willingness to face death for the sake of pleasure or desire.
  • 假寐以俟 - Pretended to sleep and waited, a term indicating a state of preparedness or anticipation.
  • 向恐麾之不去，今且虑招之弗来 - Previously, I was afraid to drive her away, now I fear she may not come at all, indicating a reversal of concern from fear of the ghost’s presence to fear of her absence.
  • 俄若有人小语曰 - Soon, as if someone whispered softly, indicating the sudden arrival or presence of a ghostly figure.
  • 据拥之就枕 - Grabbed her and held her to the pillow, indicating immediate physical contact or intimacy.
  • 女本荡妇 - The woman was originally a wanton woman, indicating her moral character or reputation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 未嫁而孕 - In classical Chinese society, an unmarried woman becoming pregnant was a grave social transgression, often leading to severe punishment or even death.
  • 父母怒缢之 - The act of strangling a daughter for premarital pregnancy was a known form of punishment in ancient China, reflecting strict Confucian moral codes.
  • 草葬 - A burial in the grass or in an unmarked grave, indicating a lack of respect or mourning, often associated with dishonored or outcast individuals.
  • 顿悟 - A term commonly found in Buddhist and Daoist texts, referring to sudden enlightenment or realization, though here used more in a psychological or emotional context.
  • 假寐 - A term from classical literature referring to the act of pretending to sleep, often used in contexts of waiting or surveillance.
  • 荡妇 - A term used to describe a woman of loose morals or promiscuous behavior, indicating the societal judgment of the female character.

Proper Nouns

  • 子 - The son, a generic term for a male character in the narrative.
  • 翁 - The father or the old man, likely the father of the son.
  • 某家 - A certain family, indicating an unspecified or anonymous family.
  • 女 - The woman or the girl, referring to the ghost or the woman who was strangled.

  • 若言为吾妇，其信然乎 - A question asking if the woman truly intends to be his wife, with '信然' meaning 'indeed so.'
  • 业已妇矣，何问焉 - Means 'I am already your wife, why ask?' with '业已' meaning 'already' and '何问焉' meaning 'why ask?'

  • 良不止为一夕之欢 - Means 'it is not just about one night's pleasure,' emphasizing the long-term commitment.

  • 妾为父母所贱 - Means 'I was despised by my parents,' reflecting a negative past experience.
  • 殁后厝之浅土 - Means 'after death, I was buried in shallow soil,' with '厝' meaning 'to bury.'
  • 霜露沁骨，灌莽缠骸 - Describes the harsh conditions of the burial site, with '霜露' meaning 'frost and dew,' '沁骨' meaning 'penetrate the bones,' '灌莽' meaning 'overgrown vegetation,' and '缠骸' meaning 'cling to the bones.'
  • 更惧为豺犬所搰 - Means 'I fear being dug up by wild dogs,' with '搰' meaning 'to dig up.'
  • 徒饱馋口 - Means 'only to satisfy the hunger of beasts,' with '馋口' meaning 'hungry mouths.'
  • 君能徙妾高阜，且深埋之 - Means 'if you can move me to a high place and bury me deeply,' with '徙' meaning 'move,' '高阜' meaning 'high place,' and '深埋' meaning 'bury deeply.'
  • 当为君鬼妻 - Means 'I will be your ghostly wife,' with '鬼妻' meaning 'ghost wife.'
  • 一切如命 - Means 'everything as you command,' indicating full obedience.
  • 鬼亦能诞育如人耶 - A question asking if ghosts can give birth like humans, with '诞育' meaning 'give birth.'
  • 凡人暴卒者，魂气凝聚，不即散 - Means 'for those who die suddenly, their spirit gathers and does not disperse immediately,' with '暴卒' meaning 'die suddenly' and '魂气凝聚' meaning 'spirit gathers.'
  • 能施能受，妊娠如常 - Means 'they can give and receive, and conceive as usual,' with '能施能受' meaning 'can give and receive' and '妊娠如常' meaning 'conceive as usual.'
  • 病而后殁，则不能矣 - Means 'if they die after being ill, then they cannot,' with '病而后殁' meaning 'die after being ill.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 暴卒 - Refers to sudden death, often associated with supernatural phenomena.
  • 魂气凝聚 - A belief in the gathering of spirit after death, which can influence the living.
  • 妊娠如常 - Reflects the idea that spirits can still engage in biological processes, a concept found in some Chinese ghost stories.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 踌躇间 - Literally 'hesitatingly' or 'in a state of hesitation,' indicating a moment of indecision or contemplation.
  • 天佑 - A phrase meaning 'divine protection' or 'heaven's favor,' often used to explain fortunate or unexplained events.
  • 操家计 - Literally 'manage household affairs,' referring to taking care of the family's daily needs and responsibilities.
  • 一菽一水 - A metaphor for the simplest and most basic necessities, often used to describe minimal but sufficient provisions.
  • 私为市绵 - Literally 'secretly buy silk,' indicating that the woman is quietly preparing for the future.
  • 暇则纺绩 - Literally 'in her spare time, she spins and weaves,' describing her industrious activities.
  • 未及飞霜 - Literally 'before the frost flies,' a poetic way of saying 'before winter arrives' or 'in time.'
  • 絮衣咸备 - Literally 'all the warm clothes are ready,' indicating that everything needed for winter is prepared.
  • 绐以天佑 - Literally 'tell it as if it were divine protection,' suggesting that the father is being deceived or misled into believing that the situation is due to divine intervention.
  • 矜人 - Literally 'boast about,' meaning to show off or brag about something.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天佑 - A common belief in ancient Chinese culture that good fortune or unusual events are often attributed to divine intervention or heavenly favor.
  • 操家计 - A traditional role for women in Chinese society, where they were responsible for managing the household and ensuring the family's well-being.
  • 一菽一水 - A reference to the simplicity and frugality often associated with virtuous or hardworking individuals.
  • 私为市绵 - An allusion to the industriousness and foresight of women in preparing for the future, often seen as a virtuous trait.
  • 絮衣咸备 - A reflection of the seasonal changes and the importance of being prepared for winter, which is a common theme in Chinese literature and daily life.
  • 绐以天佑 - A subtle criticism of the father's tendency to attribute everything to divine intervention, rather than recognizing the efforts of his daughter.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '踌躇间，子乃笑曰' introduces a moment of hesitation, followed by the son's response, which creates a contrast between uncertainty and resolution.
  • The son's statement that this is 'divine protection' is a way of explaining the unexplained, but it also sets up a contrast with the daughter's actual efforts.
  • The phrase '女虽不昼见，亦不归' is elliptical, meaning that the daughter does not appear during the day but also does not leave, suggesting a hidden presence.
  • The daughter's actions are described in a way that emphasizes her secrecy and dedication, as she works in the dark to manage household affairs.
  • The phrase '饮食以时，备具无烦预办矣' is a bit ambiguous, as it could mean that meals are prepared in advance without any trouble, or that the daughter ensures that everything is ready at the right time.
  • The daughter's explanation is cryptic, suggesting that she can stay during the day because of the son's 'yang energy' (positive male energy), but she is afraid to be seen by others.
  • The daughter's request is indirect, asking the son to secretly buy silk for her, which implies a deeper, unspoken reason.
  • The daughter's actions are described in a way that emphasizes her meticulous care for the family's clothing, ensuring that everything is clean and in good condition.
  • This phrase is poetic, indicating that the daughter has prepared everything in time before winter arrives.
  • The final phrase is a bit ambiguous, as it could mean that the daughter presents the prepared clothes to the father, telling him that it was due to divine protection, and the father does not question it.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 藻鉴有时亦取诸人间 - Means 'the standard of selection sometimes also comes from among humans,' indicating that human judgment can complement divine or celestial criteria.
  • 孝足动天 - Means 'filial piety is enough to move heaven,' emphasizing the power of filial piety to reach the divine.
  • 学足型世 - Means 'learning is sufficient to model the world,' suggesting that profound knowledge can set an example for society.
  • 修文院济济多士 - Refers to the Academy of Literature (修文院) being full of talented scholars, indicating a prestigious institution.
  • 头脑冬烘 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'stupid' or 'clueless,' suggesting a lack of intelligence or insight.
  • 材衡玉尺 - A metaphor for a standard of measurement, often used to describe a strict or high standard.

  • 忽焉花锁棘闱 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'suddenly, a beautiful but difficult examination,' suggesting an unexpected challenge.
  • 不拣金而拾翠 - Means 'not choosing gold but picking up jade,' indicating a preference for what is less obvious but more valuable.
  • 朱衣有眼 - Refers to the red-robed officials (朱衣) who have discerning eyes, suggesting that those in power can recognize true talent.
  • 董双成宁落孙山 - Refers to the mythological figure Dou Shuangcheng (董双成), who would not fall behind in the examination, indicating that true talent will be recognized.
  • 彤管无灵 - Refers to the red writing brush (彤管), which lacks divine power, suggesting that human judgment is necessary.
  • 李易安终居康了 - Refers to the poet Li Qingzhao (李清照), who ultimately settled in a peaceful place, indicating that true talent may not always be recognized immediately.
  • 载赓柏舟之句 - Refers to the continuation of the 'Bai Zhou' (柏舟) verse, a reference to a poem in the *Book of Songs* (诗经) that expresses loyalty and dedication.
  • 婺耀龙头 - Refers to the brightness of the star Weizhu (婺女星), which is associated with the dragon's head, indicating a prominent position.

  • 道德仁义之蕴 - Refers to the profound meanings of morality, virtue, and righteousness, indicating a focus on deeper philosophical and ethical content.
  • 寥寥不堪数计也 - Means 'too few to count,' suggesting that true talent is rare and exceptional.

Idiomatic Expressions

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 镜中之影自解其衣 - This describes a supernatural phenomenon where the reflection in the mirror undresses itself, which is a common motif in Chinese folklore and literature, often associated with spirits or magical objects.
  • 情不能禁 - This reflects the cultural context where strong emotional responses to beauty and art are common and sometimes lead to intimate or passionate actions.
  • 下帷欢好 - This phrase is a euphemism for intimate relations, reflecting the cultural norms and taboos surrounding such topics in classical Chinese literature.
  • 铁椟 - An iron box, which was a common method of storing valuable or dangerous items in ancient China.
  • 缄封 - Sealing tightly, which was a common practice to ensure the safety and security of important or sensitive items.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The relationship between the mirror and the sound is implicit in '立鏡於榻側，夫妇并坐听之.'
  • The description of the sound is highly evocative and metaphorical, requiring careful translation to capture the delicate and enchanting nature in '其声娇细而簌簌可动梁尘.'
  • The phrase '工雅不群' is a compound adjective that describes the melody as skillful, elegant, and unique, which must be rendered clearly in English.
  • The supernatural action must be clearly articulated in '已而镜中之影自解其衣.'
  • The description of the reflection's appearance is highly poetic and must be translated to convey the beauty and purity in '体洁白如玉.'
  • The sequence of actions must be clearly described to maintain the narrative flow in '先裸而后舞.'
  • The specific movements and gestures must be translated to convey the grace and beauty in '折腰曲腕，献眉呈身.'
  • The overall effect of the scene must be captured to convey the allure and beauty in '睹此旖旎之态.'
  • The emotional response must be translated to convey the intensity and passion in '情不能禁.'

  • This sets the scene for the timing of events: soon after, the city's evening curfew is enforced, and the town gates are closed early.

  • This sets the stage for the ceremony: the auspicious time is approaching, and the girl's parents come together to visit the house.
  • This describes Cui's thoughts and actions: she thinks the fish-shaped lock has been lowered, making it impossible to go back, and only then does she happily open the door and let them in.

Moreover, the girl was stubborn, and her parents always let her do as she pleased, so she closed the door and lived alone, and no one dared to disturb her.

  • 泉路有良朋，桂榜失吉士矣 - Metaphorical statement about the afterlife: 'In the realm of the dead, one may find good companions, but in the world of the living, one loses a virtuous scholar.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sequence describes the woman's actions in a compact form. She did not look back, smiled as she entered her residence, took her box, and handed it to Huang.
  • This clause explains what Huang should do with the box and why she is not explaining further in detail.
  • This sequence describes the woman's departure and disappearance. She wiped her sleeves and walked out directly, and there was already a sedan chair waiting at the door. She got into it and soon disappeared without a trace.
  • This clause describes Huang's reaction and his subsequent search. He found it deeply strange and visited the eastern village, but there were no signs of her parents.
  • This clause describes Huang's next action. He carried the box to the county town the next day to verify its contents.
  • This clause describes Huang's arrival at the office and the officials' announcement. He questioned the reason and became even more shocked.
  • This clause explains the magistrate's suspicion and his actions. The magistrate had been deceived many times and felt that it was supernatural, so he locked the new wife's room.
  • This clause describes the woman's words to the magistrate. She said that just received the rain and dew, and immediately treated me like a cast-off object, and asked if he could not write a poem about the autumn wind and a fan for her.
  • This clause describes the woman's demeanor while speaking. She smiled and welcomed him.
  • This clause describes the magistrate's reaction and the unexpected situation. He was shocked to the point of disbelief, quickly got up, intending to leave the house, but the door was actually locked.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是盖其英灵不泯，特借歌者以传之 - '英灵不泯' refers to the spirit or virtue remaining undiminished, while '特借歌者以传之' suggests that the story is being passed down through songs, indicating a form of oral tradition or folklore.
  • 非真学杨越公、裴晋公，作此一番豪举也 - '非真学' means 'not truly learning from,' and '作此一番豪举' refers to performing a heroic deed, suggesting that the act was not a result of deliberate imitation but rather an expression of inherent virtue.
  • 独喜之本末，颇近不经 - '独喜之本末' refers to the origin and development of a particular story or event, while '颇近不经' suggests that the story seems somewhat unbelievable or extraordinary.
  • 然而疾疫传染死者，未必皆无禄之人 - '未必皆无禄之人' means 'not necessarily all without rank or status,' implying that the victims of the epidemic were not limited to the lower classes.
  • 如是则冢中之活鬼，又不止一喜已也 - '冢中之活鬼' is a metaphor for restless spirits or ghosts, and '不止一喜已也' means 'not just one,' suggesting that there were many such spirits.
  • 可发一噱 - A phrase meaning 'can provoke a laugh,' often used to indicate something humorous or amusing.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 未識君肯援手否？ - A polite inquiry meaning 'Do you not know if you would be willing to lend a hand?'
  • 二一一力任，无难色。 - Ambiguous phrase meaning 'The second person (二) took on the responsibility with ease, showing no difficulty,' or 'The second person (二) took on the responsibility with one effort, showing no difficulty.'
  • 书生乃出金与牒，再拜而付之 - 'The scholar then presented the gold and the document, bowing twice before handing them over.'
  • 然后与少艾和烛而灭 - 'Then, with the young lady, he extinguished the candle together.'
  • 二亦目眩移时 - 'The second person (二) also experienced dizziness for a while.'
  • 四野萧森，慄不可留 - 'The surrounding wilderness was desolate and eerie, making it impossible to stay.'
  • 遂怀金亟返 - 'He then hurriedly returned home with the gold.'
  • 至家视其物，粲然白镪，真金也 - 'Upon arriving home and inspecting the item, it was clearly white and shiny, truly gold.'
  • 予不任受功，亦不任受过，得此生殖足矣，其他何恤焉？ - 'I cannot take credit for the deed, nor can I bear the blame. With this, I can sustain myself, and what else should I worry about?'
  • 竟私其金，不为市榇，又匿其牒，不以焚 - 'In the end, he kept the gold for himself, did not use it to buy a coffin, and also hid the document without burning it.'
  • 贪念已萌 - 'The desire had already taken root.'
  • 旬日，金将用尽，仍出负贩 - 'After ten days, the gold was almost spent, and he once again went out to carry goods.'
  • 途遇州役系之去，茫然不知其由 - 'On the way, he encountered a local official who detained him, and he was completely unaware of the reason.'
  • 至案则积赌某某咸在，二曾以鬼金偿其债，寻变为楮，故以妖妄讼之耳 - 'Upon arrival at the case, it was found that the person had accumulated gambling debts, and the second person (二) had used the ghost gold to pay off the debt, which later turned into paper money, hence the lawsuit was filed on the grounds of superstition.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼金 - Refers to gold obtained through supernatural means, often associated with ghosts or spirits.
  • 楮 - Refers to paper money, specifically the type used in ancient China.
  • 妖妄 - Refers to superstition or irrational behavior, often used to describe actions that defy rational explanation.
  • 讼之 - Refers to the act of filing a lawsuit or complaint.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '二一一力任，无难色。' is ambiguous in terms of its exact meaning and the role of '一' (one) in the sentence.
  • The phrase '然后与少艾和烛而灭' is ambiguous. It could mean 'extinguished the candle together' or 'extinguished the candle with the young lady.'
  • The phrase '二亦目眩移时' is ambiguous. It could mean 'experienced dizziness for a while' or 'had a dizzying experience for a while.'
  • The phrase '四野萧森，慄不可留' is ambiguous. It could mean 'making it impossible to stay' or 'causing fear that made it impossible to stay.'
  • The phrase '至家视其物，粲然白镪，真金也' is ambiguous. It could mean 'clearly white and shiny' or 'shining brightly like white gold.'
  • The phrase '予不任受功，亦不任受过，得此生殖足矣，其他何恤焉？' is ambiguous. It could mean 'with this, I can sustain myself' or 'with this, I can reproduce myself.'

  • 君但得佳处栖身，何必问堂构之所自哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'If you have found a good place to dwell, why bother to inquire about the origins of the structure?' Reflects a pragmatic or philosophical attitude.

  • 畫圖中人 - Literal meaning: 'a person from a painting.' Common expression in classical Chinese literature to describe someone who is exceptionally beautiful or graceful, often used to praise women.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 女后连举二男 - Describes a woman (likely a fox spirit) giving birth to two male offspring in quick succession.
  • 忽辞成璧欲还陇中 - Metaphorical expression where '成璧' refers to the protagonist, and '陇中' is a place name, suggesting a sudden decision to leave.
  • 坚留之，腼然曰 - '坚留之' means 'to insistently keep her,' and '腼然' describes a shy or embarrassed demeanor.
  • 妾等与人居，皆以采纳自益 - Indicates that the fox spirits benefit from living with humans by gaining nourishment and resources.
  • 寿永而道可成 - Suggests that their longevity and spiritual path are enhanced by living with humans.
  • 自与君遇，姊之意初欲以妾为饵 - Metaphorical expression where '以妾为饵' means 'to use me as bait,' indicating a manipulative intent.
  • 窃君之英华，兼以钓君之货贿 - Suggests that the fox spirits aim to exploit the protagonist's vitality and wealth.
  • 非有心为姻好也 - Indicates that the fox spirits' intentions are not genuine in terms of forming a marital bond.
  • 适值妾厄于韩卢 - Metaphorical expression where '韩卢' refers to a fierce dog, suggesting that the fox spirit was in danger.
  • 赖君驱斥，妾因铭刻不忘 - Indicates that the protagonist saved the fox spirit from danger, and she has never forgotten the act.
  • 曲成燕婉 - Describes the fox spirit's efforts to maintain a harmonious relationship.
  • 今在此数岁，两索皆雄 - Indicates that after several years, the fox spirit has given birth to two male offspring.
  • 虽可以报君，而妾之前修尽弃 - Suggests that while the fox spirit can repay the protagonist, her previous efforts have been wasted.
  • 将归山存养，不可复留 - Indicates that the fox spirit plans to return to the mountains to nurture herself and cannot stay.
  • 万勿以妾为念 - Advises the protagonist not to worry about her.
  • 富女至，相偕以返 - Indicates that another fox spirit (the '富女') arrives, and they leave together.
  • 遂不复来 - Indicates that they do not return.
  • 成璧始悟所救之狐，即其室中人 - Indicates that the protagonist realizes that the fox spirit he saved was actually a woman from his household.
  • 由此戒杀，亦不言娶，后竟以希寿终 - Indicates that the protagonist stops killing and refrains from marrying, eventually living a long life.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - Common introductory phrase for historical or biographical commentary; often translated as 'the historian says' or 'the chronicler remarks.'
  • 狐亦有无盐 - Reference to the legendary beauty Meng Jiangnu, implying that foxes have their own kind of beauty or virtue.
  • 狐中之徐吾 - Reference to Xu Wu, a historical figure known for his wisdom and prudence, suggesting that this fox possesses similar qualities.
  • 借穴壁之光 - Metaphorically means 'borrowing light from a wall' or 'using limited resources to achieve success.'
  • 竟成横金之妇 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'eventually becoming a wealthy woman,' where '横金' (hengjin) refers to a wealthy or prosperous woman.
  • 奢俭因地之语 - Reference to the idea that luxury and frugality should be adapted to one's circumstances, often associated with Confucian teachings.
  • 独贫狐也欤哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is it only poor foxes who do this?'
  • 狐其小者也 - Concluding statement meaning 'foxes are indeed small creatures.'
  • 若何迟好合而待毛、施 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why delay marriage and wait for someone like Mao or Shi?' (Mao and Shi were famous matchmakers in ancient China.)
  • 恐镜台未下，潘鬓将星，九十春光不亦大半虚掷乎 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'fear that before the mirror is lowered, one's hair will turn gray, and half of one's life will be wasted.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝为人后，不以嗣续为念，而惟佳丽是求，是诚何心 - Rhetorical question expressing disapproval. '汝为人后' means 'you being the heir,' '不以嗣续为念' means 'not thinking of continuing the lineage,' '而惟佳丽是求' means 'but only seeking beauty,' and '是诚何心' means 'what kind of heart is this.'
  • 言已就坐，盛气相向 - Describes the father's reaction after speaking. '言已就坐' means 'after finishing speaking, he sat down,' and '盛气相向' means 'with a stern and angry expression.'
  • 生伏地，屏息不敢仰视 - Describes the son's reaction to his father's anger. '生伏地' means 'the son fell to the ground,' '屏息' means 'held his breath,' and '不敢仰视' means 'dared not look up.'
  • 父将命忠行杖，生甫顿首白曰 - Describes the father's action and the son's response. '父将命忠行杖' means 'the father ordered his servant to beat him,' '生甫顿首' means 'the son immediately knelt and bowed,' and '白曰' means 'and said.'
  • 儿不肖，诚负父恩，但思室家为人生重事，脱非所愿而曲就之，实同附骨之疽 - The son's defense. '儿不肖' means 'I am unworthy,' '诚负父恩' means 'truly I have failed my father's expectations,' '但思室家为人生重事' means 'but I think marriage is a crucial matter for a person,' '脱非所愿而曲就之' means 'if it is not my wish but I have to go along with it,' and '实同附骨之疽' means 'it is truly like a cancer that clings to the bone.'
  • 望大人宽其罪，使得遂其私，则罔极之恩，益同天地矣 - The son's plea. '望大人宽其罪' means 'I hope you will forgive my fault,' '使得遂其私' means 'let me fulfill my personal desire,' '则罔极之恩' means 'then your boundless kindness,' and '益同天地矣' means 'will be even greater than heaven and earth.'
  • 崩角至流血 - Describes the son's reaction to the beating. '崩角' means 'his forehead was bruised,' and '至流血' means 'to the point of bleeding.'
  • 任若为之，予终不乐有是子 - The father's final statement. '任若为之' means 'let you do as you please,' and '予终不乐有是子' means 'I will never be happy having such a son.'
  • 拂衣径入 - Describes the father's departure. '拂衣' means 'brushing off his robe,' and '径入' means 'and went in directly.'
  • 适聆公子之论，亦年少恒情，公勿过于苛责 - The woman's intervention. '适聆公子之论' means 'just heard your son's argument,' '亦年少恒情' means 'it is also a common feeling for young people,' and '公勿过于苛责' means 'please do not be too harsh.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汝为人后，不以嗣续为念 - Refers to the traditional Chinese emphasis on continuing the family lineage through male heirs.
  • 佳丽是求 - Refers to the pursuit of beauty, which can be seen as a contrast to the more serious duty of continuing the family line.
  • 附骨之疽 - A metaphor for a serious and intractable problem, often used to describe something that is deeply harmful and difficult to cure.
  • 罔极之恩 - A phrase used to describe boundless kindness or grace, often in the context of parental love.
  • 乔梓 - A term used to describe the relationship between father and son, emphasizing the mutual respect and affection between them.

Proper Nouns

  • 汝 - You (addressing the son).
  • 父 - Father.
  • 生 - Son.
  • 忠 - Servant.
  • 妾 - Woman (referring to herself).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 如庶母礼 - Following the etiquette of a stepmother, suggesting a metaphorical or symbolic act.

  • 容色殊尤 - "容色" means "appearance" or "looks," and "殊尤" means "extraordinary" or "exceptional," emphasizing the beauty of the young women.

  • 善主中馈，无贻厥父母羞 - "善主中馈" means "be good at managing the household," and "无贻厥父母羞" means "do not bring shame to your parents," emphasizing the role and expectations of a wife.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 绿绮惭赧，若无以自容 - 绿绮 (Lüqǐ) is a person's name, possibly referring to a female character. 惭赧 means feeling ashamed or embarrassed. 若无以自容 means as if unable to bear it or as if unable to face it. This phrase conveys a sense of deep embarrassment.
  • 数以横波瞩生 - 数 (shuò) means repeatedly or often. 横波 (héng bō) is a metaphor for the eye, often used to describe a woman's gaze. 瞩 (zhǔ) means to look at or gaze. This phrase means repeatedly gazing at the protagonist with her eyes.
  • 意似许可 - 意 (yì) means intention or meaning. 似 (sì) means like or as if. 许可 (xǔkě) means to permit or approve. This phrase means as if giving approval.
  • 丽人即起，趋入报生父 - 丽人 (lìrén) means a beautiful woman. 趋入 (qūrù) means to rush in or hurry in. 报 (bào) means to report or inform. 生父 (shēngfù) means the protagonist's father. This phrase means the beautiful woman immediately rose and hurried in to report to the protagonist's father.
  • 痴阿翁怒故未息也 - 痴阿翁 (chī āwēng) means foolish old man, a term of endearment or affectionate insult. 怒 (nù) means anger. 故未息也 (gù wèi xī yě) means still not calmed down. This phrase means the foolish old man is still angry.
  • 遽命忠治青庐 - 遽 (jù) means hastily or suddenly. 命 (mìng) means to order or command. 忠 (zhōng) is a name or title, possibly referring to a servant or attendant. 治 (zhì) means to prepare or arrange. 青庐 (qīnglú) is a type of temporary structure used for weddings or other ceremonies. This phrase means hastily ordered Zhong to prepare the green tent.
  • 又出新衣一笥，俾生与绿绮俱更其故衣者 - 又出 (yòu chū) means to take out again. 新衣 (xīnyī) means new clothes. 一笥 (yī sì) means a basket or container. 俾 (bǐ) means to make or cause. 生与绿绮 (shēng yǔ lǜqǐ) means the protagonist and Lüqǐ. 俱更其故衣者 (jù gēng qí gù yī zhě) means to change their old clothes. This phrase means took out a basket of new clothes and made the protagonist and Lüqǐ change their old clothes.
  • 周匝缜密，无异慈乌 - 周匝 (zhōuzā) means thorough or all-around. 缜密 (zhěnmì) means meticulous or careful. 无异 (wúyì) means no difference or not different from. 慈乌 (cíwū) means a kind of bird, often used to symbolize filial piety. This phrase means thorough and meticulous, not different from a filial bird.
  • 生亦心窃感之 - 生 (shēng) means the protagonist. 亦 (yì) means also. 心窃 (xīnqiè) means secretly or in one's heart. 感之 (gǎn zhī) means to feel grateful or moved. This phrase means the protagonist also secretly felt grateful.
  • 将夕，堂中皆燃巨烛 - 将夕 (jiāngxī) means near evening. 堂中 (tángzhōng) means in the hall. 皆 (jiē) means all. 燃 (rán) means to light. 巨烛 (jùzhú) means large candles. This phrase means near evening, all the candles in the hall were lit.
  • 生父始出受礼 - 生父 (shēngfù) means the protagonist's father. 始 (shǐ) means finally or at last. 出 (chū) means to come out. 受礼 (shòulǐ) means to receive the ceremony. This phrase means the protagonist's father finally came out to receive the ceremony.
  • 谓生曰 - 谓 (wèi) means to say to. 生 (shēng) means the protagonist. 曰 (yuē) means said. This phrase means told the protagonist.
  • 此皆婢子好事，老夫颇不耐此 - 此 (cǐ) means this. 皆 (jiē) means all. 婢子 (bìzi) means a servant girl. 好事 (hǎoshì) means to do good deeds or to be well-meaning. 老夫 (lǎofū) means old man, a term of self-reference. 颇不耐此 (pō bù nài cǐ) means very much unable to tolerate this. This phrase means all this is because of the servant girl's good intentions, but the old man is very much unable to tolerate this.
  • 已而交拜成礼 - 已而 (yǐ'ér) means after a while. 交拜 (jiāobài) means to exchange bows. 成礼 (chénglǐ) means to complete the ceremony. This phrase means after a while, they exchanged bows to complete the ceremony.
  • 送入庭后一华屋，供具丰美，锦绣灿陈，均皆丽人之赐 - 送入 (sòngrù) means to send into. 庭后 (tíng hòu) means behind the hall. 一华屋 (yī huáwū) means a beautiful room. 供具 (gōngjù) means provisions or supplies. 丰美 (fēngměi) means abundant and beautiful. 锦绣 (jǐnxuǎn) means brocade or embroidered fabric. 灿陈 (càntíng) means brightly displayed. 均皆 (jūn jiē) means all. 丽人之赐 (lìrén zhī cì) means the gift of the beautiful woman. This phrase means sent them into a beautiful room behind the hall, where the provisions were abundant and beautiful, the brocade and embroidery were brightly displayed, all of which were gifts from the beautiful woman.
  • 生促绿绮就枕，代为解衣 - 生 (shēng) means the protagonist. 促 (cù) means to urge or hasten. 绿绮 (lǜqǐ) means Lüqǐ. 就枕 (jiùzhěn) means to go to bed. 代为 (dàiwèi) means to do on behalf of. 解衣 (jiěyī) means to take off clothes. This phrase means the protagonist urged Lüqǐ to go to bed and took off her clothes on her behalf.
  • 莺雏燕乳，不禁啼红 - 莺雏 (yīngchú) means young swallows. 燕乳 (yànlǔ) means young sparrows. 不禁 (bùjīn) means unable to resist. 啼红 (tíhóng) means to cry red tears, a metaphor for crying. This phrase means the young swallows and sparrows could not resist crying red tears.
  • 欢毕，生始展问里族 - 欢毕 (huānbì) means after the joy had ended. 生 (shēng) means the protagonist. 始 (shǐ) means then. 展问 (zhǎnwèn) means to ask. 里族 (lǐzú) means hometown or clan. This phrase means after the joy had ended, the protagonist then asked about her hometown and clan.
  • 答曰 - 答 (dá) means to answer. 曰 (yuē) means said. This phrase means answered.
  • 妾与姑皆胡姓，父母远任剑南，妾姊妹赖姑以生，故婚媾一唯其命，父母不必与闻也 - 妾 (qiè) means I (a humble term for a woman). 与 (yǔ) means and. 姑 (gū) means aunt. 皆 (jiē) means both. 胡姓 (húxìng) means the surname Hu. 父母 (fùmǔ) means parents. 远任 (yuǎnrèn) means far away in service. 剑南 (jiàn nán) means a region in Sichuan. 妹姊 (mèizǐ) means sisters. 赖 (lài) means depend on. 生 (shēng) means live. 故 (gù) means therefore. 婚媾 (hūngòu) means marriage. 一 (yī) means completely. 唯 (wéi) means only. 其 (qí) means her. 命 (mìng) means fate. 父母 (fùmǔ) means parents. 不必 (bùbì) means not necessary. 与闻 (yǔwén) means to know about. This phrase means I and my aunt are both surnamed Hu. My parents are far away in service in Sichuan. My sisters and I depend on my aunt to live, so our marriage is entirely determined by her, and our parents do not need to know about it.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生与绿绮盥濯讫 - Sheng and Lvqi have finished washing. '盥濯' (guanzhuo) is an archaic term for washing or cleansing.
  • 易髫而髻 - '易' (yi) means to change, '髫' (tiao) refers to a child's hair style, and '髻' (ji) is a bun or coiled hairstyle. Together, it means changing from a child's hairstyle to an adult's.
  • 往谒舅姑 - '谒' (ye) means to visit, '舅' (jiu) is the husband's father, and '姑' (gu) is the husband's mother. '舅姑' (jiu gu) is a respectful term for the husband's parents.
  • 生亦入省见 - '省' (sheng) means to visit or pay respects, and '见' (jian) means to see. Together, it means Sheng also went to pay respects.
  • 宜遄归 - '遄' (chuan) means to hurry or return quickly. '宜' (yi) means should or ought to. Together, it means 'should return quickly.'
  • 若母倚闾断肠矣 - '若母' (ruo mu) means 'your mother,' '倚闾' (yi lu) means 'leaning on the gate,' and '断肠' (duan chang) means 'heartbroken.' Together, it means 'your mother is leaning on the gate, heartbroken.'
  • 长跽涕泣不愿行 - '长跽' (chang ji) means to kneel for a long time, '涕泣' (ti qi) means to cry, and '不愿行' (bu yuan xing) means 'do not want to leave.' Together, it means 'kneeling for a long time, crying, and not wanting to leave.'
  • 此岂汝久居之所耶 - '岂' (qi) is an interrogative particle, '汝' (ru) means 'you,' '久居' (jiu ju) means 'long stay,' and '之所' (zhi suo) means 'place.' Together, it means 'Is this where you are supposed to stay for a long time?'
  • 父出白金二笏 - '笏' (hu) is an ancient ceremonial tablet. '白金' (bai jin) means silver. Together, it means 'the father took out two silver tablets.'
  • 将去奉母赡妻 - '将去' (jiang qu) means 'to take away,' '奉母' (feng mu) means 'to support the mother,' and '赡妻' (shan qi) means 'to provide for the wife.' Together, it means 'to take away to support the mother and provide for the wife.'
  • 多与恐不胜任也 - '多与' (duo yu) means 'too much,' '恐' (kong) means 'fear,' and '不胜任' (bu shen ren) means 'cannot handle.' Together, it means 'fearing that too much will not be manageable.'
  • 亦嘱绿绮，善事乃姑 - '嘱' (zhu) means 'to instruct,' '善事' (shan shi) means 'to treat well,' and '乃姑' (nai gu) means 'your mother-in-law.' Together, it means 'also instructed Lvqi to treat her mother-in-law well.'
  • 勿倚痴娇如在我处 - '勿' (wu) means 'do not,' '倚痴娇' (yi chi jiao) means 'rely on foolishness and pretense,' and '如在我处' (ru zai wo chu) means 'as if you were here.' Together, it means 'do not rely on foolishness and pretense as if you were here.'
  • 衣襦数十事 - '衣襦' (yi ru) means 'clothes and undergarments,' and '数十事' (shu shi) means 'several dozen items.' Together, it means 'several dozen items of clothes and undergarments.'
  • 亟遣之归 - '亟' (ji) means 'hastily,' and '遣之归' (qian zhi gui) means 'send them back home.' Together, it means 'hastily sent them back home.'
  • 泣不能出声 - '泣' (qi) means 'to cry,' and '不能出声' (bu neng chu sheng) means 'cannot speak.' Together, it means 'crying and unable to speak.'
  • 无何，忠入劝驾曰 - '无何' (wu he) means 'shortly after,' '忠' (zhong) is a name, '入劝驾' (ru quan jia) means 'entered to persuade,' and '曰' (yue) means 'said.' Together, it means 'shortly after, Zhong entered to persuade and said.'
  • 车马已具，少主夫妇可行矣 - '车马已具' (che ma yi ju) means 'the carriage and horses are ready,' '少主' (shao zhu) means 'young master,' '夫妇' (fu fu) means 'husband and wife,' and '可行矣' (ke xing yi) means 'can go now.' Together, it means 'the carriage and horses are ready, the young master and his wife can go now.'
  • 畜产恋父，竟不念而母耶 - '畜产' (chu chan) is a derogatory term for a son, '恋父' (lian fu) means 'loves the father,' '竟' (jing) means 'still,' '不念' (bu nian) means 'does not think of,' and '而母' (er mu) means 'your mother.' Together, it means 'a son loves the father, still does not think of your mother?'
  • 仆马相俟 - '仆' (pu) means 'servant,' '马' (ma) means 'horse,' and '相俟' (xiang si) means 'awaiting.' Together, it means 'servants and horses awaiting.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - A conventional opening phrase used by historians or chroniclers to introduce a commentary or reflection.
  • 甚矣，父母之劬劳靡尽也 - A rhetorical exclamation meaning 'How great is the toil and effort of parents!' *劬劳* refers to the hard work and suffering of parents, especially in raising children.
  • 冥漠之中 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'in the realm of the unseen' or 'beyond the visible world,' often used to refer to the afterlife or spiritual realm.
  • 厥子亦有狐妻 - A literal translation would be 'his son also has a fox wife,' but this likely refers to a supernatural or mythological context.
  • 李与狐殆世为秦晋者矣 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'Li and the fox are almost like the two ancient states of Qin and Jin, who were often at war but also had alliances.' This suggests a complex relationship between the human and supernatural worlds.
  • 非狐之贤淑 - A negative statement meaning 'it is not due to the fox's virtue and beauty,' implying that the relationship is not based on merit.
  • 其父既长负向平之债 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'his father had already incurred a debt to Xiang Ping,' referring to a historical figure known for his filial piety and obligations.
  • 子亦终乏范蠡之缘 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'his son also lacked the fortune of Fan Li,' referring to the legendary Chinese statesman and businessman who became wealthy and retired.
  • 其不为良朋之笑柄也，几希 - A rhetorical statement meaning 'how little chance they have of becoming a laughingstock among good friends!' *几希* means 'very little' or 'hardly any.'
  • 痴狐 - A term that can be translated as 'foolish fox' or 'simple-minded fox,' suggesting a character with limited intelligence but still capable of charm.
  • 性憨而善媚 - A descriptive phrase meaning 'he/she is simple-minded but good at flattery,' indicating a character who is naive but skilled at pleasing others.
  • 公讳畹 - A formal way of introducing a person by their courtesy name, *Wan*, which is a respectful way to refer to someone.
  • 戊辰进士 - A title indicating that the person passed the imperial examinations in the year of the Earth Dragon (戊辰), a specific year in the Chinese zodiac.
  • 以冏卿致仕 - A phrase meaning 'he retired as a high-ranking official,' *冏卿* is a title for a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
  • 年仅六旬 - A phrase meaning 'he was only in his sixties,' indicating his age.
  • 颇以声色自娱 - A phrase meaning 'he greatly enjoyed himself with music and women,' indicating a hedonistic lifestyle.
  • 极意搜罗 - A phrase meaning 'he sought to collect everything,' suggesting a passion for gathering rare and valuable items.
  • 终鲜尤物 - A phrase meaning 'he ultimately lacked something exceptional,' indicating a sense of dissatisfaction despite his wealth and status.
  • 心不免歉然 - A phrase meaning 'his heart could not help but feel disappointed,' indicating a sense of regret or dissatisfaction.
  • 行及负郭 - A phrase meaning 'he walked to the outskirts of the city,' *负郭* refers to the area near the city walls.
  • 桃英将落 - A phrase meaning 'the peach blossoms were about to fall,' indicating the end of spring.
  • 菜花渐开 - A phrase meaning 'the vegetable flowers were gradually blooming,' indicating the arrival of summer.
  • 结子桃花颜色失 - A phrase meaning 'the peach blossoms, which had borne fruit, lost their color,' indicating the passage of time and the fading of beauty.
  • 沿畦蔬菜蕊空香 - A phrase meaning 'the vegetables along the ridges had empty petals but still smelled fragrant,' indicating the persistence of beauty even as things change.
  • 可怜一样闲风月 - A phrase meaning 'pitifully, the idle pleasures of life are the same,' indicating a sense of futility or repetition.
  • 难向枝头觅海棠 - A phrase meaning 'it is hard to find a hibiscus on the branches,' indicating the difficulty of finding something rare or beautiful.
  • 忽闻篱落间有嘻笑声 - A phrase meaning 'suddenly, he heard laughter coming from behind the fence,' indicating a sudden and unexpected event.
  • 且恒探半身以相望 - A phrase meaning 'and they would often peek out half their bodies to look at him,' indicating a curious and playful interaction.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 父母之劬劳靡尽也 - A reference to the traditional Chinese value of filial piety and the hard work of parents in raising their children.
  • 冥漠之中 - A reference to the afterlife or spiritual realm, often associated with ancestor worship and the belief in the continuity of life after death.
  • 李与狐殆世为秦晋者矣 - A reference to the historical states of Qin and Jin, which were often at war but also had alliances, suggesting a complex relationship between the human and supernatural worlds.
  • 向平之债 - A reference to Xiang Ping, a historical figure known for his filial piety and obligations, indicating a sense of duty and responsibility.
  • 范蠡之缘 - A reference to Fan Li, a legendary Chinese statesman and businessman who became wealthy and retired, indicating a sense of fortune and success.
  • 戊辰 - A reference to the year of the Earth Dragon, a specific year in the Chinese zodiac, indicating a specific historical context.
  • 进士 - A title indicating that the person passed the imperial examinations, indicating a high level of education and status.
  • 冏卿 - A title for a high-ranking official in the imperial court, indicating a high level of authority and influence.
  • 致仕 - A term for retirement, indicating a transition from active service to a more leisurely life.
  • 六旬 - A term for someone in their sixties, indicating a specific age range.
  • 声色自娱 - A term for enjoying oneself with music and women, indicating a hedonistic lifestyle.
  • 搜罗 - A term for collecting rare and valuable items, indicating a passion for gathering and preserving.
  • 尤物 - A term for something exceptional or rare, indicating a sense of value and uniqueness.
  • 负郭 - A term for the area near the city walls, indicating a specific location.
  • 桃英 - A term for peach blossoms, indicating the beauty and fragility of nature.
  • 菜花 - A term for vegetable flowers, indicating the persistence of beauty even as things change.
  • 结子 - A term for bearing fruit, indicating the passage of time and the cycle of life.
  • 沿畦 - A term for the ridges where vegetables are planted, indicating a specific agricultural context.
  • 蕊 - A term for petals, indicating the beauty and fragility of flowers.
  • 闲风月 - A term for idle pleasures, indicating a sense of futility or repetition.
  • 海棠 - A term for hibiscus, indicating the difficulty of finding something rare or beautiful.
  • 篱落 - A term for a fence, indicating a boundary or separation.
  • 嘻笑 - A term for laughter, indicating a sense of joy or amusement.
